name: AI/ML Engineering
description: Machine learning, deep learning, training, inference, and MLOps
axioms: Bias-variance tradeoff: model complexity trades off between underfitting and overfitting
Gradient descent: follow negative gradient to minimize loss function
Backpropagation: chain rule applied layer-by-layer to compute gradients
Universal approximation: sufficiently wide neural network can approximate any function
No free lunch: no single algorithm is best for all problems
Data quality > model complexity: garbage in, garbage out
reasoning_patterns: Data pipeline: collect → clean → feature engineer → split → train → evaluate → deploy
Hyperparameter tuning: grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization
Transfer learning: pre-train on large dataset, fine-tune on target task
Ensemble methods: combine multiple models (bagging, boosting, stacking)
A/B testing: measure model improvement in production with statistical rigor
boundary_conditions: Does not cover ML theory (convergence proofs, generalization bounds) in depth
Does not cover specific frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) API details
Computer vision and NLP are specialized applications
Reinforcement learning is a separate subfield with distinct challenges
key_references: Bishop, C. Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning
Sutton & Barto. Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
Murphy, K. Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective
canonical_truths: Transformers (attention mechanism) are the dominant architecture for language tasks
Scaling laws: performance improves predictably with more data, compute, and parameters
Overfitting is the default — regularization is required, not optional
Feature engineering is often more important than model selection
Production ML is 80% data engineering, 20% modeling
